---
title: MSI Protocol
description: Modified, Shared, Invalid — the basic coherence protocol
sidebar:
  order: 1

import MSIWalker from '../../../../components/visualizations/MSIWalker';

## Three States

Each cache line is in exactly one of three states:

| State | Meaning | Can read? | Can write? | Memory up-to-date? |
|-------|---------|-----------|------------|---------------------|
| **M** (Modified) | This cache has the only valid copy; it's been written | Yes | Yes | No (cache has newer data) |
| **S** (Shared) | Clean copy; other caches may also have copies | Yes | No (must upgrade first) | Yes |
| **I** (Invalid) | Not in cache or stale | No | No | — |

## State Transitions

### On a Read (PrRd)

| Current State | Action | Next State |
|---------------|--------|------------|
| M | Read hit | M |
| S | Read hit | S |
| I (miss) | Issue BusRd; if another cache has M, it flushes → both go S | S |

### On a Write (PrWr)

| Current State | Action | Next State |
|---------------|--------|------------|
| M | Write hit | M |
| S | Issue BusUpgr → invalidate other sharers | M |
| I (miss) | Issue BusRdX → get line, invalidate others | M |

### On Snooping Bus Signals

| Observed Signal | My State | Action | My New State |
|-----------------|----------|--------|--------------|
| BusRd | M | Flush data to bus + memory | S |
| BusRd | S | No action | S |
| BusRdX / BusUpgr | M | Flush data to bus + memory | I |
| BusRdX / BusUpgr | S | — | I |

## The Key Invariant

At any time, for any cache line, exactly one of these is true:
- **One** cache holds the line in M, and all others are I
- **Multiple** caches hold the line in S (and memory is up-to-date)
- **All** caches are I (nobody has the line)

This invariant is what guarantees coherence: there's never a situation where two caches have conflicting data.

## Interactive: Apply Your Own Operations

Pick a read/write and a core, click Apply. The widget shows the resulting state vector and the bus traffic. The bottom invariant box confirms MSI's key safety property after every step.

<MSIWalker client:only="react" />

:::caution[Common misconception: "MSI is correct because the protocol diagram is correct."]
MSI is correct *only because of the bus's serializing property*. The protocol diagram doesn't enforce anything by itself — it relies on the assumption that every cache sees the same sequence of bus transactions in the same order. On a system with a non-totally-ordered interconnect (e.g., a mesh NoC), the same per-cache state machine would be unsafe; you need ordering at the interconnect layer or a directory to re-impose serialization. MSI's textbook diagram quietly assumes "shared bus" — directory protocols look superficially similar but carry significantly more bookkeeping.
:::

## Worked Example: Three Cores, One Cache Line

Trace the state and bus traffic for a sequence on line A, starting with everyone in I. Each row is one operation; we track who has the line in what state.

| # | Operation | Bus | Core 0 | Core 1 | Core 2 |
|---|-----------|-----|--------|--------|--------|
| 0 | initial | — | I | I | I |
| 1 | Core 0 reads A | BusRd | **S** (memory supplies) | I | I |
| 2 | Core 1 reads A | BusRd | S | **S** | I |
| 3 | Core 2 reads A | BusRd | S | S | **S** |
| 4 | Core 0 writes A | BusUpgr → Inv | **M** | I | I |
| 5 | Core 1 reads A | BusRd → flush by Core 0 | S (flushed, mem now fresh) | **S** | I |
| 6 | Core 2 writes A | BusRdX → Inv | I | I | **M** |

Five bus transactions for six accesses — and that **single write at step 4** by itself broadcast invalidations to *both* other caches even though they were only reading. Now imagine 32 cores were all sharing the line in S: that one write costs 31 invalidation messages.

This is the classic argument for **read-mostly data being a coherence bargain** and **shared-mutable data being a coherence disaster**. MESI's E state will save one BusUpgr on step 4 *only if Core 0 was the sole reader to begin with* — which it isn't here (three sharers).

## MSI Limitations

MSI has an inefficiency: when a cache reads a line that nobody else has, it goes to **S** even though it's the only copy. If it later writes to that line, it must first issue a BusUpgr (unnecessary bus traffic). The **MESI** protocol fixes this with an **Exclusive** state.
